questions.q: Three ingredients required for thunderstorm formation?
choices: Wind, rain, cold temperature
Moisture, lifting mechanism, atmospheric instability
Fog, pressure change, high humidity
Cold front, warm front, occluded front
questions.explanation: Three ingredients: adequate moisture in the lower atmosphere, a lifting mechanism (fronts, surface heating, orographic), and atmospheric instability.

questions.q: AIRMET Zulu warns of?
choices: IFR conditions and mountain obscuration
Moderate turbulence and low-level wind shear
Moderate icing and freezing level information
Severe turbulence hazardous to all aircraft
questions.explanation: AIRMET Zulu covers moderate icing not associated with thunderstorms and provides freezing level information. Sierra = IFR/mtns. Tango = moderate turbulence and LLWS.

questions.q: Temp/dewpoint spread of 3 degrees C or less indicates?
choices: Severe turbulence likely
Fog or low clouds likely to develop
Thunderstorm activity imminent
Clear VFR conditions ahead
questions.explanation: When temperature and dewpoint are within 3 degrees C, the air is near saturation. Fog, mist, or low clouds are likely to form.

questions.q: What type of front produces the most intense but shortest-duration weather?
choices: Warm front
Cold front
Stationary front
Occluded front
questions.explanation: Cold fronts move faster and produce narrow bands of intense precipitation and thunderstorms followed by rapid clearing.

questions.q: What does 9900 mean in a winds aloft forecast?
choices: Winds at 9900 feet
Winds at 99 knots variable
Light and variable winds
Data unavailable
questions.explanation: In the winds aloft (FB) forecast, 9900 means light and variable winds — speed less than 5 knots.

questions.q: VFR visibility minimum for Class G airspace below 1200 AGL during the day?
choices: 3 SM
1 SM
1/2 SM
Clear of clouds only
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.155: Class G airspace below 1200 AGL during the day requires only 1 statute mile visibility and clear of clouds.

questions.q: METAR cloud layer BKN025 — what does this indicate?
choices: Broken at 25000 ft MSL
Broken at 2500 ft AGL
Scattered at 2500 ft AGL
Broken at 250 ft AGL
questions.explanation: METAR cloud heights are in hundreds of feet AGL. BKN025 = broken layer at 2500 ft AGL. A broken layer (5/8 to 7/8 coverage) defines the ceiling.

questions.q: At which thunderstorm stage does rainfall first reach the surface?
choices: Cumulus stage
Mature stage
Dissipating stage
Pre-frontal stage
questions.explanation: Mature stage: both updrafts and downdrafts present; precipitation first reaches the surface. Most hazardous stage.

questions.q: VFR minimums inside Class B airspace?
choices: 3 SM and 500 below 1000 above 2000 horizontal
1 SM and clear of clouds
3 SM and clear of clouds
5 SM and 1000 below 1000 above 1 SM horizontal
questions.explanation: 14 CFR 91.155: Class B requires 3 SM visibility and clear of clouds. No specific cloud distance required — unique to Class B.

questions.q: Primary hazard of a microburst?
choices: Heavy rainfall causing hydroplaning
Extreme low-level wind shear with outflow exceeding 45 kts causing rapid airspeed and altitude loss
Ice crystal formation at altitude
Severe turbulence above 10000 ft
questions.explanation: A microburst produces outflow exceeding 45 knots. Aircraft flying through encounters headwind then tailwind causing rapid airspeed and altitude loss — most dangerous on approach or departure.

questions.q: Most dangerous type of structural icing and why?
choices: Rime ice — forms most quickly
Clear ice — dense, hard to remove, severely disrupts airfoil shape
Mixed ice — unpredictable
Frost — forms on ground
questions.explanation: Clear ice forms from large supercooled droplets spreading before freezing — dense, transparent, hard to remove, severely alters airfoil shape.

questions.q: AIRMET Tango warns of?
choices: IFR conditions
Moderate turbulence, sustained surface winds 30 kts or more, non-convective LLWS
Moderate icing
Severe icing hazardous to all aircraft
questions.explanation: AIRMET Tango: moderate turbulence, sustained surface winds of 30 knots or greater, and non-convective low-level wind shear.

questions.q: Non-convective SIGMET validity period?
choices: 2 hours
4 hours
6 hours
12 hours
questions.explanation: Non-convective SIGMETs are valid up to 4 hours. Hurricane/tropical cyclone SIGMETs: 6 hours. Convective SIGMETs: 2 hours.

questions.q: What type of fog forms over a cooler surface and requires wind to develop?
choices: Radiation fog
Advection fog
Upslope fog
Steam fog
questions.explanation: Advection fog requires horizontal movement of warm moist air over a cooler surface. Unlike radiation fog it requires wind and is common along coastlines.

questions.q: What is density altitude?
choices: Altimeter reading when set to 29.92 in Hg
Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature — determines aircraft performance
Altitude AGL for obstacle clearance
Height above sea level corrected for humidity only
questions.explanation: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature and humidity. High DA = less dense air = degraded aircraft performance.

questions.q: Two conditions required simultaneously for structural airframe icing?
choices: Cold temperatures and strong winds
Visible moisture AND temperature at or below 0 degrees C
Below minus 20 C only
Rain and any below-freezing temperature
questions.explanation: Structural icing requires: (1) visible moisture such as clouds, rain, or drizzle, AND (2) temperature at or below 0 degrees C.

questions.q: ISA standard temperature lapse rate?
choices: 1 degree C per 1000 ft
2 degrees C per 1000 ft
3 degrees C per 1000 ft
1 degree F per 1000 ft
questions.explanation: The International Standard Atmosphere lapse rate is 2 degrees C (3.5 degrees F) per 1000 ft up to the tropopause.

questions.q: TAF code PROB40 means?
choices: 40-knot probability winds
40 percent probability the forecast conditions will occur during the time period
Probability of 40-knot gusts
TAF valid for 40 hours
questions.explanation: PROB40 in a TAF indicates a 40 percent probability that the listed conditions will occur during the specified time period.

questions.q: Winds aloft forecasts are issued how many times per day?
choices: Once daily
Twice daily
Four times daily
Every 6 hours
questions.explanation: Winds aloft (FB) forecasts are issued twice daily, based on 00Z and 12Z upper-air observation data.

questions.q: What does AIRMET Sierra cover?
choices: Moderate turbulence
IFR conditions and mountain obscuration
Moderate icing
Severe turbulence for light aircraft
questions.explanation: AIRMET Sierra covers IFR conditions (ceiling below 1000 ft and/or visibility below 3 miles over 3000 sq mi or more) and mountain obscuration.

questions.q: At 9000 ft MSL in IMC with freezing level at 8000 ft — primary structural hazard?
choices: Carburetor ice
Structural airframe icing — above freezing level means below 0 C in visible moisture
Instrument icing only
No hazard — you are above the freezing level
questions.explanation: At 9000 ft you are above the 8000 ft freezing level, meaning temperatures are below 0 C. Combined with visible moisture (IMC), both conditions for structural icing are present.

questions.q: Conditions most favorable for radiation fog formation?
choices: Strong winds, overcast sky, high relative humidity
Clear skies, calm winds, high relative humidity, long nights in fall or winter
Warm moist air moving over cold ocean water
Strong onshore wind over cold coastal waters
questions.explanation: Radiation fog: clear sky (maximum radiation), calm winds, high humidity, long nights (fall/winter). Ground cools air to its dewpoint.

questions.q: What does a lenticular cloud indicate to a pilot?
choices: Approaching thunderstorm activity
Mountain wave activity — rotor zone and severe turbulence possible on the leeward side
Approaching warm front
Moderate icing conditions at that level
questions.explanation: Lenticular (lens-shaped) stationary clouds form at mountain wave crests on the leeward side. The rotor zone below wave crests can contain extremely violent turbulence.

questions.q: PIREPs categorize turbulence. Moderate turbulence means?
choices: Minor discomfort but objects stay in place
Unsecured objects may dislodge, occupants feel strain against belts, difficult walking, aircraft control reduced but maintained
Aircraft is temporarily out of control
Engine damage or failure risk is high
questions.explanation: Moderate turbulence: unsecured objects dislodge, occupants strain against seatbelts, difficult walking, airspeed fluctuations 15 to 25 knots. Aircraft control reduced but maintained throughout.

questions.q: Rapid pressure drop over 2 hours ahead of an approaching cold front indicates?
choices: Improving conditions and rapid clearing
Potential for stronger frontal weather, thunderstorms, gusty winds at passage
Stable conditions only
Overnight radiation fog likely
questions.explanation: Rapid pressure falls ahead of a cold front indicate intensifying low pressure and suggest stronger-than-normal frontal weather.

questions.q: What is wind shear and at what phase of flight is it most dangerous?
choices: Any wind — most dangerous at cruise altitude
Change in wind speed or direction over a short distance — most dangerous at low altitude during approach and departure
Only relevant in and near thunderstorms
A gust front — only dangerous on the ground
questions.explanation: Wind shear is a change in wind speed or direction over a short distance. Most hazardous at low altitude near thunderstorms, fronts, and temperature inversions during approach and departure.

questions.q: What is a temperature inversion and what can it cause?
choices: Normal lapse rate — no special hazard
Temperature increases with altitude instead of decreasing — can trap fog near surface and cause low-level wind shear
Temperature drops rapidly — associated only with thunderstorms
Equal temperature at all altitudes
questions.explanation: A temperature inversion occurs when temperature increases with altitude instead of decreasing. Inversions trap moisture causing low ceilings and visibility. They also create low-level wind shear.

questions.q: VFR on top at 8500 ft when clouds below close in. No instrument rating. Best immediate action?
choices: Descend through the clouds — it is an emergency
Continue VFR on top until you find a gap
Immediately contact ATC, declare the situation, and request assistance — radar vectors to VFR, SVFR, or nearest suitable airport
Climb to Class A airspace
questions.explanation: This is an urgency or emergency situation. Immediately contact ATC, declare the situation, and request radar vectors to VFR conditions or a Special VFR clearance. Attempting to descend VFR into IMC without an instrument rating is extremely dangerous.

questions.q: Carburetor icing most likely at what conditions?
choices: Below minus 20 C only
Between 20 and 70 degrees F with high relative humidity — even on clear days
Only when flying through rain or visible moisture
Only at night
questions.explanation: Carburetor ice can form between 20 and 70 degrees F with high relative humidity. The venturi effect and fuel vaporization can lower carb temperatures up to 70 degrees F — ice even on warm clear days.

questions.q: A PIREP of MOD ICE indicates?
choices: Minor frost on windshield — not a hazard
Moderate structural icing — a change of course or altitude is recommended
Severe icing requiring immediate action
Ice only on airspeed probe
questions.explanation: Moderate icing (MOD ICE) in a PIREP indicates ice accumulation is such that a change of course or altitude is recommended. Ice accumulates rapidly enough to be problematic if flight continues.

questions.q: What is a convective SIGMET and how long is it valid?
choices: Same as a regular SIGMET — 4 hours
A SIGMET specifically for severe convective weather (thunderstorms) covering an area 3000 sq mi or more, or embedded thunderstorms, or tornadoes, or hail 3/4 inch or greater — valid 2 hours
A SIGMET issued by pilots only — valid until conditions end
A SIGMET for volcanic ash only — valid 6 hours
questions.explanation: Convective SIGMETs (WST) cover severe or extreme convective weather including tornadoes, hail at or above 3/4 inch diameter, severe or extreme turbulence, severe icing, and widespread thunderstorms. Issued hourly (or special), valid 2 hours.

questions.q: What is a PIREP and what format does it follow?
choices: Pilot Route Evaluation Plan — informal pilot checklist
Pilot Report — standardized report from a pilot in flight providing actual weather observations; includes: UA/UUA, location, time, altitude, aircraft type, sky, temp, wind, turbulence, icing, remarks
Pressure Indication Reference for En-route Planning — a weather product
Published Instrument Route Entry Procedure — an IFR document
questions.explanation: PIREPs (Pilot Reports) provide real-time weather data from pilots in flight. Format: type (UA=routine, UUA=urgent), location (3-letter identifier), time (UTC), altitude (FL), aircraft type (AC), sky (SK), temperature (TA), wind (WV), turbulence (TB), icing (IC), remarks (RM).

questions.q: What is the standard atmosphere pressure at sea level in inches of mercury?
choices: 29.82 in Hg
29.92 in Hg
30.02 in Hg
28.92 in Hg
questions.explanation: The International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) defines sea level standard pressure as 29.92 in Hg (1013.25 millibars). This is the altimeter setting used to obtain pressure altitude.

questions.q: A METAR reports winds as 27015G25KT. What does this mean?
choices: Winds from 270 degrees at 15 knots with gusts to 25 knots
Winds from 270 degrees at 25 knots gusting to 15 knots
Winds variable from 270 to 150 degrees at 25 knots
Winds from 270 at 15 knots with visibility 25 SM
questions.explanation: In a METAR, wind format is: direction (270 = from the west), speed (15 knots), G = gusts, gust speed (25 knots). So 27015G25KT = wind from 270 degrees at 15 knots with gusts to 25 knots.

questions.q: What is the difference between IFR and LIFR conditions?
choices: There is no difference — IFR and LIFR are the same
IFR is ceiling 500 to below 1000 ft and/or visibility 1 to below 3 SM. LIFR (Low IFR) is ceiling below 500 ft and/or visibility below 1 SM.
IFR is any ceiling below 3000 ft. LIFR is any ceiling below 1000 ft.
LIFR is only used in military aviation
questions.explanation: Aviation weather flight categories: VFR (ceiling above 3000 ft, vis above 5 SM), MVFR (ceiling 1000-3000 ft and/or vis 3-5 SM), IFR (ceiling 500 to below 1000 ft and/or vis 1 to below 3 SM), LIFR (ceiling below 500 ft and/or vis below 1 SM).

questions.q: What is a cold front occlusion and a warm front occlusion?
choices: These are the same weather phenomenon called an occluded front
Cold front occlusion: the cold front catches and lifts the warm front — colder air behind. Warm front occlusion: the air ahead of the warm front is colder — less common in the US.
Occluded fronts do not have subtypes
An occluded front is simply a stationary front that has changed direction
questions.explanation: An occluded front forms when a cold front overtakes a warm front. In a cold front occlusion (more common), the air behind the cold front is colder than the air ahead of the warm front. In a warm front occlusion, the air ahead of the warm front is colder.

questions.q: What does a METAR SKY cover of OVC010 indicate?
choices: Overcast clouds at 10000 ft
Overcast clouds at 1000 ft AGL — this is the ceiling
Overcast clouds at 100 ft AGL
10 percent overcast cloud coverage
questions.explanation: In a METAR, SKC=sky clear, FEW=1-2 oktas, SCT=3-4 oktas scattered, BKN=5-7 oktas broken (defines ceiling), OVC=8 oktas overcast (defines ceiling). Heights are in hundreds of feet AGL. OVC010 = overcast at 1000 ft AGL.

questions.q: What is the freezing level and how does it affect flight planning?
choices: The altitude at which cabin temperature reaches 0 degrees C
The altitude at which the ambient air temperature is 0 degrees C — above this altitude temperatures are below freezing and structural icing is possible in visible moisture
The altitude at which fuel begins to freeze in the tanks
The altitude at which carburetor ice is most likely
questions.explanation: The freezing level is the altitude at which the ambient air temperature equals 0 degrees C. Flight above the freezing level in visible moisture creates icing conditions for the airframe. Multiple freezing levels can exist if temperature inversions are present.

questions.q: What is a pressure gradient and how does it affect wind speed?
choices: Pressure gradient is the difference in pressure between engine intake and exhaust — affects engine performance

Pressure gradient is the rate of pressure change per unit horizontal distance. A steep pressure gradient (closely spaced isobars) indicates stronger winds. A shallow gradient (widely spaced isobars) indicates light winds.
Pressure gradient only affects altimeter readings
Pressure gradient is only relevant for IFR flight planning
questions.explanation: The pressure gradient is the rate of change of pressure over horizontal distance, shown by spacing of isobars on surface analysis charts. Closely spaced isobars indicate a steep gradient and strong winds. Widely spaced isobars indicate light winds.

questions.q: What is orographic lift and what type of clouds does it produce?
choices: Lift created by hot asphalt runways in summer
Lift created when air is forced to rise over terrain — produces orographic clouds including cap clouds, lenticular clouds, and can trigger significant thunderstorm activity
Lift created by jet engines — only applies to jets
Lift created by temperature inversions
questions.explanation: Orographic lift occurs when moving air is forced upward by terrain. As air rises it cools, potentially reaching the dewpoint and forming clouds. This can produce cap clouds, lenticular clouds (wave), and can trigger convective activity on the windward side.

questions.q: What does a center weather advisory (CWA) provide?
choices: A replacement for the GFA for a specific center's area
An unscheduled weather advisory issued by an ARTCC meteorologist for conditions that don't meet SIGMET criteria but may affect the safety of IFR operations or VFR flight in the area — valid 2 hours
A daily weather briefing for center aircraft only
A forecast issued by the NWS for oceanic operations
questions.explanation: Center Weather Advisories (CWAs) are issued by ARTCC meteorologists for weather that doesn't meet SIGMET criteria but may affect safety. They cover the ARTCC area of responsibility and are valid for up to 2 hours.

questions.q: What is frost and why is it dangerous to aircraft?
choices: Frost is essentially the same as light freezing drizzle — it forms from supercooled droplets and melts rapidly after takeoff, posing no significant lift penalty for aircraft with a wing loading above 10 pounds per square foot

Frost is ice crystals that form on aircraft surfaces when the surface temperature is at or below the dewpoint AND below freezing. Even light frost can significantly disrupt airflow over the wing and reduce lift by up to 30%.

Rime ice is ice crystals that form on aircraft surfaces when the surface temperature is above the dewpoint but below freezing. Even light rime ice can significantly disrupt airflow over the wing and reduce lift by up to 30%.

Frost is supercooled water droplets that freeze on aircraft surfaces when the airframe descends through a temperature inversion below freezing. Even light frost can significantly disrupt airflow over the wing and reduce lift by up to 30%.
questions.explanation: Frost forms when a surface is at or below the dewpoint and below 0 degrees C. Even a thin layer of frost significantly disrupts the smooth flow of air over the wing by increasing surface roughness, reducing lift up to 30 percent and increasing drag. All frost must be removed before flight.

questions.q: What is a stability index and what does a high/low value indicate?
choices: Atmospheric stability indices such as the Showalter Index measure the potential for turbulence development. A positive Showalter Index indicates instability and clear-air turbulence potential. Negative values indicate convectively stable air.

Atmospheric stability indices such as the Lifted Index measure the potential for thunderstorm development. A negative Lifted Index indicates instability and thunderstorm potential. Positive values indicate stable air.

Atmospheric stability indices such as the K-Index measure the potential for structural icing at altitude. A positive K-Index above 20 indicates high instability and icing potential. Negative values indicate stable, ice-free conditions.

Atmospheric stability indices such as the CAPE value measure the potential for radiation fog development. A negative CAPE value indicates unstable air with high fog potential. Positive CAPE values indicate stable and likely clear conditions.
questions.explanation: Stability indices like the Lifted Index compare the temperature of a parcel of air lifted to 500 mb with the actual temperature at that level. Negative values indicate the parcel is warmer than its environment (unstable), suggesting convective potential. Values below -6 suggest severe thunderstorms.

questions.q: What weather phenomenon is associated with a rapidly falling barometric pressure?
choices: Improving VFR conditions
Approaching low pressure system — typically deteriorating weather, increasing winds, and possible precipitation
Fog formation
Stable high pressure settling in
questions.explanation: A rapidly falling barometric pressure indicates an approaching low pressure system. Low pressure systems are associated with rising air, clouds, precipitation, and generally deteriorating weather conditions.

questions.q: What is a squall line and how does it affect VFR flight planning?
choices: A line of thunderstorms that can extend hundreds of miles and contain severe weather, embedded thunderstorms, and violent turbulence — VFR pilots should consider it a no-go boundary
A minor weather system similar to a warm front
A line of fog banks along a coastline
A boundary between different air masses with no significant weather
questions.explanation: A squall line is a non-frontal line of active thunderstorms that can extend hundreds of miles ahead of a cold front. It contains the most severe weather of any convective system and should not be penetrated by VFR (or most IFR) aircraft. It requires complete avoidance.

questions.q: What is an isobar and how is it used on a surface analysis chart?
choices: A line connecting points of equal temperature
A line connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure — isobars show wind patterns, frontal systems, and help identify areas of strong pressure gradient (strong winds)
A line connecting points of equal humidity
A line connecting points of equal cloud cover
questions.explanation: Isobars are lines on a surface analysis chart connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure. Wind flows roughly parallel to isobars (modified by friction near the surface). Closely spaced isobars indicate a steep pressure gradient and strong winds.

questions.q: What is the Coriolis force and how does it affect wind direction in the northern hemisphere?
choices: Coriolis force has no effect on aviation weather

The Coriolis effect causes winds to deflect to the right in the northern hemisphere due to the Earth's rotation — results in clockwise circulation around high pressure and counterclockwise around low pressure
Coriolis force causes winds to deflect left in the northern hemisphere
Coriolis force only affects high-altitude jet streams
questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect, caused by Earth's rotation, deflects moving air to the right in the northern hemisphere. This results in clockwise circulation around high-pressure systems and counterclockwise circulation around low-pressure systems (Buys-Ballot's Law: stand with your back to the wind, low pressure is to your left in northern hemisphere).

questions.q: What is wind shear on approach and what are the warning signs?
choices: Wind shear on approach is only a problem for large aircraft

Wind shear on approach warning signs include: airspeed fluctuations of 15 knots or more, unusual pitch attitudes required to maintain glidepath, rapid change in descent rate, and loss of visual perspective on runway
Wind shear on approach always produces turbulence so it is easy to detect
Wind shear on approach only occurs in thunderstorms
questions.explanation: Wind shear on approach can be subtle and rapidly dangerous. Warning signs: significant airspeed fluctuations (15+ kts), unusual power changes needed to stay on glidepath, rapid VSI changes, runway perspective changes. The recommended action is a go-around if significant wind shear is encountered.

questions.q: What is MVFR and what ceiling and visibility define it?
choices: Minimum VFR — ceiling at or above 3000 ft and visibility at or above 5 SM
Marginal VFR — ceiling 1000 to 3000 ft and/or visibility 3 to 5 SM
Military VFR — only applies to military operations
Medium VFR — ceiling between 1000 and 10000 ft
questions.explanation: MVFR (Marginal VFR) is a flight category defined as ceiling 1000 to 3000 ft and/or visibility 3 to 5 SM. These conditions are legal for VFR flight but represent marginal margins. Many accidents occur in MVFR conditions as weather deteriorates to IFR.

questions.q: How does humidity affect aircraft performance and density altitude?
choices: Humidity has no effect on aircraft performance
High humidity increases density altitude because water vapor is lighter than dry air, replacing denser oxygen and nitrogen molecules — this reduces engine power and aerodynamic performance
High humidity decreases density altitude because water is heavier than air
Humidity only affects turbine engines, not piston aircraft
questions.explanation: High relative humidity increases density altitude because water vapor (molecular weight 18) is less dense than dry air (molecular weight ~29). This displaces oxygen and reduces engine power output and propeller/wing efficiency. Hot, humid days at high elevation airports can create extremely high density altitude.

questions.q: What is the significance of the tropopause for VFR pilots?
choices: The tropopause marks the altitude at which standard lapse rate reverses and temperature starts rising — VFR pilots must note it because above this level cloud formation is impossible and VFR visibility minima no longer apply.

The tropopause is the boundary between the troposphere (where all weather occurs) and the stratosphere — most severe weather occurs below it; pilots planning high-altitude VFR flights should understand it marks the top of the weather-containing atmosphere

The tropopause is the altitude at which standard atmospheric pressure drops below 500 mb — VFR pilots crossing it experience sudden altimeter errors requiring resetting the Kollsman window to the ISA standard of 29.92 in Hg.

The tropopause defines the transition from subsonic to supersonic airflow around the airframe — VFR pilots operating near it risk compressibility buffet and must reduce airspeed to below Mmo as published in the aircraft flight manual.
questions.explanation: The tropopause marks the top of the troposphere where virtually all weather occurs. Temperature stops decreasing and may increase or stabilize. Most severe weather is below it. The tropopause height varies from about 25,000 ft at the poles to 60,000 ft at the equator.

questions.q: What cloud type is associated with thunderstorm activity?
choices: Cirrus
Stratus
Cumulonimbus
Altocumulus
questions.explanation: Cumulonimbus (CB) is the thunderstorm cloud. It is a vertical development cloud extending from low levels to the upper troposphere (up to 60,000+ ft). Never fly into or through a CB. Avoid by at least 20 nm.

questions.q: What does a lenticular cloud indicate to a pilot?
choices: Approaching warm front with gradual deterioration
Mountain wave turbulence — severe rotor turbulence possible below the cloud on the leeward side
Good VFR conditions with stable air
Convective activity — thunderstorms likely nearby
questions.explanation: Lenticular (lens-shaped) clouds form at the crest of mountain waves and remain stationary as air flows through them. The rotor zone below the wave crest can contain extremely violent turbulence. Avoid lenticular clouds and the area below them.

questions.q: What is the key difference between stable and unstable air regarding cloud type and precipitation?
choices: Stable air produces cumuliform clouds and showery precipitation; unstable produces stratiform clouds and steady precipitation
Stable air produces stratiform clouds and steady precipitation; unstable air produces cumuliform clouds and showery precipitation
Both types produce the same clouds but at different altitudes
Stability only affects turbulence, not cloud type
questions.explanation: Stable air: stratiform clouds, smooth air, steady precipitation, poor visibility in fog/haze. Unstable air: cumuliform clouds, turbulence, showery/heavy precipitation, good visibility in precipitation. This distinction helps predict weather type from synoptic patterns.

questions.q: Frost forms when the surface temperature is at or below the dewpoint AND below what temperature?
choices: 32 degrees F only if humidity is above 90%
0 degrees C (32 degrees F). Even a thin layer reduces lift up to 30% and must be removed before flight.
Minus 10 degrees C
Any temperature below standard ISA
questions.explanation: Frost forms when a surface is at or below the dewpoint temperature AND at or below 0 degrees C. Unlike structural icing in flight, frost forms on the ground. Even a light coat of frost disrupts airflow, reduces lift up to 30%, and increases drag. All frost must be removed before flight.

questions.q: What is the Convective Outlook (AC) and when is it useful?
choices: A forecast issued by Aviation Weather Center identifying areas of potential mountain wave turbulence and severe clear-air turbulence risk for up to 6 hours. Useful for tactical route planning when turbulence avoidance is a significant concern.

A forecast issued by the Storm Prediction Center identifying areas of potential convective activity and severe thunderstorm risk for up to 8 days. Useful for strategic trip planning when convective weather is a concern.

A forecast issued by the National Hurricane Center identifying areas of potential tropical convective activity and severe weather risk for up to 5 days. Useful for strategic coastal trip planning when tropical weather systems are present.

A forecast issued by local NWS offices identifying areas of potential convective activity and flash flood risk for up to 3 days. Useful for strategic low-altitude trip planning when convective weather and terrain flooding are a concern.
questions.explanation: The Convective Outlook (AC) is issued by the Storm Prediction Center and identifies areas of slight, enhanced, moderate, and high risk for severe thunderstorm development. It is a planning tool for the next 1-8 days and should be consulted before any cross-country when convective weather is possible.

questions.q: Cirrus clouds at high altitude with wispy streaks indicate what weather trend?
choices: Good VFR conditions will persist for many hours
A possible approaching warm front — cirrus typically appear 24-48 hours before a warm front surface passage
Thunderstorm development in the next few hours
High winds aloft only — no surface weather significance
questions.explanation: Cirrus clouds form at high altitudes (above 20,000 ft) and are composed of ice crystals. Their presence can indicate an approaching warm front, which typically reaches a given area 24-48 hours after cirrus is first spotted. Also associated with jet stream turbulence (CAT).

questions.q: What weather conditions does AIRMET Sierra specifically warn about?
choices: Moderate turbulence and low-level wind shear
IFR conditions — ceilings below 1,000 ft and/or visibility below 3 SM over a large area, or mountain obscuration
Moderate icing and freezing level information
Severe turbulence that could affect all aircraft
questions.explanation: AIRMET Sierra covers: IFR conditions (ceiling below 1,000 ft and/or visibility below 3 SM affecting 3,000 or more square miles) and mountain obscuration. It is particularly relevant to VFR pilots who may not have an instrument rating to fly through IFR conditions.

questions.q: What is the Surface Analysis Chart used for in preflight planning?
choices: It shows forecast winds aloft for the next 12 hours

It shows current surface weather including pressure systems, frontal locations, and isobars — not a forecast. Used to identify current frontal positions, pressure gradient, and overall weather pattern.
It shows a forecast of ceiling and visibility for the route
It is the same as the TAF but in graphic form
questions.explanation: The Surface Analysis Chart shows current (not forecast) surface weather conditions including: high and low pressure centers, frontal boundaries, isobars (lines of equal pressure), and precipitation areas. Updated every 3 hours. Closely spaced isobars indicate a steep pressure gradient and strong winds.
